title: On the Multilinear Restriction and Kakeya conjectures

conj:1.1.summary: The linear restriction conjecture: for $S$ with non-vanishing Gaussian curvature, $q>\tfrac{2d}{d-1}$ and $p'\le\tfrac{d-1}{d+1}q$, $\|\mathcal{E}g\|_{L^q(\mathbb{R}^d)}\lesssim\|g\|_{L^p(U)}$. The classical open problem this paper attacks the multilinear analogue of.

conj:1.3.summary: The multilinear restriction conjecture: under transversality, $\|\prod_{j=1}^d\mathcal{E}_jg_j\|_{L^{q/d}(\mathbb{R}^d)}\lesssim\prod_j\|g_j\|_{L^p(U_j)}$ for $q\ge\tfrac{2d}{d-1}$, $p'\le\tfrac{d-1}{d}q$. The sharp statement; thm:1.16 proves it up to an $R^\epsilon$ loss.

conj:1.7.summary: The linear Kakeya conjecture: for a $\delta$-separated set of $\delta\times1$-tubes $\mathbb{T}$, $\|\sum_{T\in\mathbb{T}}\chi_T\|_{L^q(\mathbb{R}^d)}\lesssim\delta^{(d-1)/q}(\#\mathbb{T})^{1/q}$ for $q>\tfrac{d}{d-1}$. Equivalent to the Kakeya/Besicovitch dimension conjecture.

conj:1.8.summary: The multilinear Kakeya conjecture: for $d$ transversal families of $\delta\times1$-tubes, $\|\prod_{j=1}^d(\sum_{T_j\in\mathbb{T}_j}\chi_{T_j})\|_{L^{q/d}(\mathbb{R}^d)}\lesssim\prod_j(\delta^{d/q}\#\mathbb{T}_j)$ for $\tfrac{d}{d-1}\le q\le\infty$. thm:1.15 proves this up to an arbitrarily small $\delta^{-\epsilon}$ loss.

thm:1.15.summary: Near-optimal multilinear Kakeya: for $\tfrac{d}{d-1}<q\le\infty$ and transversal families $\mathbb{T}_1,\dots,\mathbb{T}_d$, $\|\prod_j(\sum_{T_j\in\mathbb{T}_j}\chi_{T_j})\|_{L^{q/d}}\lesssim\prod_j(\delta^{d/q}\#\mathbb{T}_j)$, and with an arbitrarily small $\delta^{-\epsilon}$ loss at the endpoint $q=\tfrac{d}{d-1}$. The multilinear Kakeya conjecture, modulo $\epsilon$.

thm:1.16.summary: Near-optimal multilinear restriction: for $\epsilon>0$, $q\ge\tfrac{2d}{d-1}$, $p'\le\tfrac{d-1}{d}q$, $\|\prod_{j=1}^d\mathcal{E}_jg_j\|_{L^{q/d}(B(0,R))}\lesssim_\epsilon R^\epsilon\prod_j\|g_j\|_{L^p(U_j)}$ for all $R\ge1$. The multilinear restriction conjecture up to $R^\epsilon$ — the input later used by Bourgain–Guth and by Bourgain–Demeter's decoupling proof.

prop:2.1.summary: Restriction-from-Kakeya transfer with self-improvement: $\mathcal{R}^*(\dots;\alpha) + \mathcal{K}^*(\dots;\epsilon) \implies \mathcal{R}^*(\dots;\tfrac\alpha2+\tfrac\epsilon4)$. Iterated (induction on scales / parabolic rescaling), it bootstraps any multilinear restriction estimate down to the $R^\epsilon$ statement using multilinear Kakeya.

lem:2.2.summary: Reformulation: the multilinear restriction estimate $\mathcal{R}^*(\dots;\alpha)$ is equivalent to $\|\prod_j\widehat{f_j}\|_{L^{q/d}(B(0,R))}\lesssim R^{\alpha-d/2}\prod_j\|f_j\|_2$ for $f_j$ Fourier-supported in the $R^{-1}$-neighborhood of the pieces. Lets the transfer be run on $L^2$ functions.

prop:3.1.summary: Heat-flow monotonicity (Loomis–Whitney case): for positive semidefinite $A_j$ and the Gaussian-flow quantities $f_j(t,x)=\int e^{-\pi\langle A_j(x-v_jt),x-v_jt\rangle}d\mu_j(v_j)$, the weighted integral $\int\prod_j f_j(t,x)^{p_j}dx$ is monotone in $t$ when $\sum_j p_jA_j$ is the Loomis–Whitney configuration. The engine of the whole proof.

cor:3.2.summary: The (continuous, multilinear) Loomis–Whitney / Brascamp–Lieb inequality $\int\prod_j f_j(1,x)^{p_j}dx\le(\det A_*)^{-1/2}\prod_j\|\mu_j\|^{p_j}$, obtained by comparing the monotone quantity of prop:3.1 at $t=1$ and $t=0$.

prop:4.1.summary: Perturbed heat-flow monotonicity: the same Gaussian-flow argument works when the matrices $M_j$ only satisfy $p_1M_1+\cdots+p_nM_n>_{pd}M_j$ (a strict-domination condition) rather than the exact Loomis–Whitney relation, at the cost of an $O(\epsilon)$ error. The version actually applied to families of tubes with varying orientations.

cor:4.2.summary: Perturbed Loomis–Whitney inequality: $\int\prod_j f_j(1,x)^{p_j}dx\le(1+O(\epsilon))(\det M_*)^{-1/2}\prod_j\|\mu_j\|^{p_j}$ under the strict-domination hypothesis. The local building block summed over scales to give thm:1.15.

lem:4.4.summary: Exact evaluation of the second-derivative ('trace') quantity arising in the heat-flow computation, in terms of $\det M_*$, the means $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{w}_j)$, and projection-defect variances. The identity that makes the monotonicity inequality tight.

lem:4.5.summary: Rigidity: for $d\ge3$, if $A_1,\dots,A_d$ are rank-$(d-1)$ nonnegative matrices with spanning kernels and $\tfrac1{d-1}\sum A_i\ge A_j$ for all $j$, then $A_d$ is determined by $A_1,\dots,A_{d-1}$. Pins down when the perturbed inequality can be sharp.

prop:4.6.summary: Equality analysis: if the perturbed Loomis–Whitney quantity with $p=(\tfrac1{d-1},\dots)$ is monotone for all positive measures supported on sets $W_j$, then either $d=2$ or each $W_j$ is a single matrix with $\tfrac1{d-1}\sum A_i\ge A_j$ — the rigid configuration of lem:4.5.

thm:5.1.summary: Lower levels of multilinearity: for $n\le d$ transversal families, $\|\prod_{j=1}^n(\sum_{T_j\in\mathbb{T}_j}\chi_{T_j})\|_{L^{q/n}(\mathbb{R}^d)}\lesssim\prod_j(\delta^{d/q}\#\mathbb{T}_j)$ for $q>\tfrac n{n-1}$. The $k$-linear ($k<d$) Kakeya estimate, reduced to the $d$-linear case thm:1.15.

thm:6.2.summary: Variable-coefficient multilinear restriction: for Hörmander-type oscillatory operators $S_\lambda^{(j)}$ satisfying transversality and a curvature condition, $\|\prod_{j=1}^d S_\lambda^{(j)}g_j\|_{L^{q/d}}\lesssim_\epsilon\lambda^\epsilon\prod_j\lambda^{-d/q}\|g_j\|_{L^p}$. The phase-space generalization of thm:1.16.

thm:6.5.summary: Variable-coefficient multilinear Kakeya: for tube families with curved cores satisfying transversality, $\|\prod_{j=1}^d\sum_{T_j\in\mathbb{T}_j}\chi_{T_j}\|_{L^{q/d}}\lesssim_\epsilon\delta^{-\epsilon}\cdots$ for $q\ge\tfrac d{d-1}$. The phase-space generalization of thm:1.15 — the heat-flow argument is local, so it transfers.

prop:6.8.summary: Self-improvement for variable-coefficient Kakeya: $\mathcal{K}_c^*(\dots;\alpha)+\mathcal{K}^*(\dots;\epsilon)\implies\mathcal{K}_c^*(\dots;\tfrac\alpha2+\tfrac\epsilon2)$. The induction-on-scales step for the curved setting.

prop:6.9.summary: Variable-coefficient restriction-from-Kakeya transfer: $\mathcal{R}_c^*(\dots;\alpha)+\mathcal{K}_c^*(\dots;\epsilon)\implies\mathcal{R}_c^*(\dots;\tfrac\alpha2+\tfrac\epsilon4+\epsilon_0)$. The phase-space analogue of prop:2.1.

thm:7.1.summary: Application to the joints problem: the number of $\theta$-transverse joints determined by $n$ lines in $\mathbb{R}^3$ is $O_\epsilon(n^{3/2+\epsilon}\theta^{-1/2-\epsilon})$. A combinatorial-geometry consequence of multilinear Kakeya thm:1.15.

lem:8.2.summary: Polynomial extrapolation: if $\int\prod_j f_j^{p_j}$-type identities $G_1=G_2$ hold (as polynomials in the exponents $p$) on a suitable set, they extend; the algebraic device that lets the $t=0$/$t=1$ comparison in the heat-flow argument be made rigorous for all admissible exponents.

edges.note: Multilinear restriction is bootstrapped from any starting estimate down to the $R^\epsilon$ statement by iterating the restriction-from-Kakeya transfer.
The Kakeya input fed into the transfer is the near-optimal multilinear Kakeya estimate.
The transfer is run on the $L^2$-reformulation of the restriction estimate.
The $\mathcal{K}^*$ hypothesis in the implication is supplied by thm:1.15.
Multilinear Kakeya is obtained by summing the perturbed Loomis–Whitney inequality over scales (induction on scales / rescaling).
...applied in its inequality form.
The perturbed monotonicity is the strict-domination relaxation of the exact Loomis–Whitney heat-flow monotonicity.
The monotonicity inequality is established via the exact second-derivative (trace) identity.
The $t=0\,/\,t=1$ comparison across all admissible exponents is justified by the polynomial-extrapolation lemma.
Comparing the monotone quantity at $t=1$ and $t=0$ yields the continuous Loomis–Whitney / Brascamp–Lieb inequality.
Same comparison in the perturbed setting.
The equality analysis is driven by the rank-$(d-1)$ rigidity lemma.
The $k$-linear ($k<d$) Kakeya estimate is reduced to the $d$-linear case by a transversality/projection argument.
The variable-coefficient Kakeya estimate runs the same (local) heat-flow monotonicity argument.
...combined with the curved-setting induction-on-scales self-improvement.
Variable-coefficient restriction is bootstrapped from variable-coefficient Kakeya, mirroring thm:1.16/thm:1.15.
...via the phase-space restriction-from-Kakeya transfer.
The joints bound is extracted from the multilinear Kakeya estimate.
thm:1.15 establishes the multilinear Kakeya conjecture up to an arbitrarily small loss.
thm:1.16 establishes the multilinear restriction conjecture up to $R^\epsilon$.
The multilinear restriction conjecture is the transversal companion of (and known to recover much of) the linear restriction conjecture.
Likewise on the Kakeya side.

conjectures.description: The four restriction/Kakeya conjectures, linear and multilinear, that frame the paper.

main_theorems.description: Near-optimal multilinear Kakeya and multilinear restriction.

transfer_and_induction_on_scales.description: The equivalence of multilinear restriction and multilinear Kakeya and the self-improving (induction-on-scales) transfer that boots either down to an $\epsilon$-loss statement.

heat_flow_monotonicity.description: The Gaussian-flow monotonicity argument: the exact Loomis–Whitney/Brascamp–Lieb case, its strict-domination perturbation, the trace identity, the polynomial-extrapolation device, and the equality/rigidity analysis.

extensions_and_applications.description: Lower levels of multilinearity, the variable-coefficient (Hörmander) generalizations, and the joints-problem application.
